agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposal to reduce delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU/UK markets serving B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure-only change — it is a fundamental alteration to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and data lifecycle with cascading effects across every stakeholder group.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Record Lifecycle / Data Retention Policy — intersects Compliance, Billing, Customer Service, and Partner Integration bounded contexts",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete audit trail of a delivery: creation, pickup, transit events, proof of delivery, exceptions, and resolution. Serves as the legal record of service performance." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Evidence that a shipment was delivered — signature, photo, GPS stamp. Required for dispute resolution and invoicing. Has independent legal significance beyond the delivery record." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Period", "definition": "The duration for which records must be stored to satisfy legal, regulatory, contractual, and operational requirements. Determined by the LONGEST applicable obligation, not the shortest." },
      { "term": "Right of Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)", "definition": "A data subject's right to request deletion of personal data, which does NOT override legal obligations to retain records for compliance purposes (Art. 17(3)(b))." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Dependency", "definition": "The reliance of business customers on the platform's delivery data to satisfy their own regulatory and audit obligations — a contractual and trust commitment." },
      { "term": "Statutory Retention Obligation", "definition": "A legally mandated minimum period for which certain business records must be preserved, violation of which constitutes a regulatory offense." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records that serve as tax-relevant documentation (invoices, proof of commercial transactions) must be retained for the minimum period required by applicable tax law — 6 years in the UK (HMRC), 10 years in Germany (AO §147), 7 years in the Netherlands, varying by EU member state.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Statutory tax retention requirements are legal obligations. Violation exposes the company to fines, failed audits, and criminal liability. No VP or policy update can override the law.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record linked to a tax-relevant transaction may be deleted before the applicable statutory retention period has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Proof of Delivery records must be retained for the full limitation period for contract and consumer claims — 6 years in the UK (Limitation Act 1980), up to 10 years in some EU jurisdictions.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "POD is the primary evidence in delivery disputes and chargebacks. Deleting it within the legal limitation window destroys the company's ability to defend against claims, directly causing financial loss.",
      "testable_assertion": "No Proof of Delivery record may be deleted before the expiry of the longest applicable limitation period for the delivery's jurisdiction."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Customs and cross-border shipping records within the EU/UK must be retained for a minimum of 3 years (Union Customs Code Art. 51), and often longer under national implementing legislation.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Customs authorities can audit cross-border transactions years after the fact. Missing records result in penalties, duty reassessments, and potential loss of authorized economic operator (AEO) status.",
      "testable_assertion": "No cross-border delivery record may be deleted before the applicable customs retention period has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "B2B customers who contractually depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting must receive data export or continued access for the duration specified in their service agreements, regardless of internal retention policy changes.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a contractual obligation that could theoretically be renegotiated, but unilateral deletion without notice or migration constitutes breach of contract.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record accessible to a B2B customer under an active service agreement may be deleted without that customer having been given adequate notice and an export/migration path."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Consumer dispute resolution requires access to delivery records for at least 2 years from delivery date (EU Consumer Rights Directive — 2-year conformity period) and potentially longer under national law.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Consumers have statutory rights to raise delivery disputes within legally defined windows. The platform must retain evidence to process these disputes. This is a consumer protection obligation.",
      "testable_assertion": "No B2C delivery record may be deleted before the consumer dispute resolution window for its jurisdiction has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Data retention periods must be determined per-record based on the LONGEST applicable legal, regulatory, and contractual obligation — not by a single blanket policy.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Different records carry different obligations (tax, customs, consumer, contractual). A flat 90-day policy cannot satisfy jurisdiction-specific and record-type-specific requirements without violating at least one.",
      "testable_assertion": "The system must evaluate each delivery record against all applicable retention rules and apply the longest required retention period before any deletion occurs."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Active dispute or claim filed on day 89 — record auto-deleted on day 90", "expected_behavior": "Records under active dispute, investigation, or legal hold must NEVER be auto-deleted regardless of retention policy. A litigation hold must override any TTL." },
    { "scenario": "B2B customer runs quarterly compliance report on day 91, expecting historical delivery data", "expected_behavior": "Customer's compliance process breaks silently. They fail their own audit. Contractual liability falls on the platform." },
    { "scenario": "Cross-border delivery between Germany and UK — which retention period applies?", "expected_behavior": "The LONGEST applicable retention period across all relevant jurisdictions must be used. For DE/UK this means minimum 6 years (UK tax) or 10 years (German AO §147)." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC or EU tax authority requests delivery records for a transaction from 4 years ago", "expected_behavior": "Records must be producible. Failure to produce constitutes a regulatory violation. Under 90-day policy, these records would have been destroyed 3+ years ago." },
    { "scenario": "Consumer files chargeback 60 days after delivery, investigation takes 45 days, POD needed on day 105", "expected_behavior": "POD must still be available. Under 90-day policy, the evidence needed to dispute the chargeback would be gone, resulting in automatic financial loss." },
    { "scenario": "GDPR data subject access request (DSAR) for delivery from 30 days ago — then 100 days ago", "expected_behavior": "At 30 days, data exists and must be provided. At 100 days under the proposed policy, data is gone — but GDPR does not require deletion of data needed for legal compliance. The current 7-year policy is legally defensible; 90 days is not." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Direct financial exposure includes: (1) Tax authority penalties for missing records — up to 100% of tax owed in some EU jurisdictions; (2) Inability to defend chargebacks — average logistics chargeback is €50-500, multiplied across all deliveries older than 90 days; (3) Breach of contract liability to B2B customers whose compliance processes break; (4) Potential loss of AEO status affecting customs processing speed and costs. Storage cost savings are trivially small compared to a single regulatory fine or audit failure.",
    "operational": "CRITICAL. Customer service loses access to delivery history after 90 days, crippling dispute resolution. B2B partner integrations that query historical data will fail silently. Compliance team loses ability to respond to regulatory inquiries. Internal analytics and trend analysis loses 98% of its historical dataset.",
    "user": "HIGH IMPACT on both segments. B2C consumers lose ability to reference past deliveries, raise late disputes, or get support for issues discovered after 90 days. B2B customers — who explicitly use the platform's data for compliance reporting — face their own audit failures and regulatory exposure, likely triggering churn and legal action.",
    "risk": "EXISTENTIAL-TIER REGULATORY RISK. Operating in the EU and UK without complying with statutory retention obligations is not a business risk — it is a legal violation. GDPR does not mandate deletion at 90 days; it requires deletion when there is no lawful basis to retain. Tax, customs, and consumer protection laws PROVIDE that lawful basis for years, not days. The framing of this as 'no business logic changes' is dangerously incorrect.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Legal & Compliance Team", "impact": "Directly exposed to regulatory non-compliance. Will be unable to respond to tax audits, customs inquiries, or legal discovery requests for any delivery older than 90 days." },
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Lose access to delivery data they depend on for their own compliance reporting. Likely constitutes breach of service agreements. High churn risk and potential litigation." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "Lose delivery history and evidence for disputes filed after 90 days. Degraded customer experience and reduced trust." },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax Team", "impact": "Unable to produce records for tax audits. Direct exposure to penalties and reassessments across every EU/UK jurisdiction of operation." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Cannot investigate or resolve any delivery issue older than 90 days. Support quality collapses for any non-trivial investigation." },
      { "group": "Data / Analytics Team", "impact": "Loses virtually all historical data for trend analysis, demand forecasting, route optimization, and performance benchmarking." },
      { "group": "Executive Leadership", "impact": "Bears ultimate liability for regulatory non-compliance. Personal liability risk under certain EU member state laws for directors who authorize destruction of legally required records." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 90,
    "reasoning": "The regulatory landscape for data retention in EU/UK logistics is well-established and unambiguous. Tax retention requirements (6-10 years), customs record obligations (3+ years), and consumer protection windows (2+ years) are statutory and non-negotiable. The proposed 90-day retention period violates ALL of these. High confidence because the business rules cited are grounded in specific, named legislation — not interpretation.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK HMRC 6-year tax record retention requirement",
      "German AO §147 10-year retention for commercially relevant documents",
      "EU Union Customs Code 3-year minimum for customs records",
      "EU Consumer Rights Directive 2-year conformity period",
      "B2B contractual dependency on delivery data access",
      "GDPR Article 17(3)(b) exemption for legal compliance retention"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact retention periods for every individual EU member state — vary by country and require legal counsel review",
      "Specific contractual terms in existing B2B agreements — need contract review to confirm exact obligations"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform processes deliveries that are commercially/tax relevant transactions, not just tracking metadata",
      "B2B service agreements include provisions for data access or do not explicitly limit it to 90 days",
      "The platform has not already implemented a separate archival system that would preserve legally required data outside the proposed deletion scope"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' and 'infrastructure optimization only.' This is factually incorrect. Changing data retention from 7 years to 90 days in an EU/UK logistics platform fundamentally alters the company's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations to B2B customers, ability to resolve consumer disputes, and capacity to respond to regulatory audits. This is a BUSINESS POLICY change with severe legal implications, not an infrastructure optimization.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal & Compliance", "B2B Customers", "B2C Consumers", "Finance", "Customer Support", "Executive Leadership"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the proposal entirely — maintain 7-year retention as-is",
        "B: Implement tiered retention — archive cold data to cheaper storage (S3 Glacier, etc.) to reduce costs while preserving legal compliance",
        "C: Implement jurisdiction-aware retention — auto-delete only records that have exceeded ALL applicable retention obligations for their jurisdiction and record type",
        "D: Proceed with 90-day deletion after full legal review confirms no statutory obligations are violated (extremely unlikely to be confirmed)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — tiered storage. This achieves the stated goal (reduce storage costs) without violating any legal obligation. Move records older than 90 days to cold/archive storage at ~90% cost reduction. Records remain retrievable for compliance, disputes, and audits. This is a standard infrastructure optimization pattern that does not require changing retention policy at all."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion destroys tax-relevant records years before statutory retention periods expire (6-10 years depending on EU member state)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion destroys Proof of Delivery within the legal limitation period for contract claims (6+ years), eliminating the platform's ability to defend against chargebacks and disputes", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion destroys customs records before the minimum 3-year retention period under EU Union Customs Code", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion unilaterally removes data that B2B customers depend on for compliance reporting, likely breaching service agreements", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "90-day auto-deletion removes consumer delivery records within the statutory dispute resolution window (2+ years)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "A flat 90-day TTL ignores the fundamental requirement to evaluate retention per-record against all applicable legal obligations", "business_rule_broken": "BR-6" },
    { "violation": "The proposal is mischaracterized as 'no business logic changes' — this framing, if accepted, would bypass legal and compliance review of a change that directly affects regulatory obligations", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1, BR-2, BR-3, BR-4, BR-5, BR-6" }
  ]
}
